FOREWORD 


These  two   volumes   of  biographies   and   indexes  bring  to   a  close   a  reference 
work  ir_  eight   volumes  on  California  Literature  that  was  begun  six  years   ago. 
A  "Bibliography  of  California  Literature;    Fiction,  Poetry,  Drama,"   in  three 
volumes,   was   brought   out   in   1938,    and    ''Criticism  of  California  Literature,    a 
Digest   and  Bibliography,"   also   in  three  volumes,   was  released    in  1940.      These 
earlier  publications  were  distributed   as   gifts  to  many   libraries   in  the 
United   States,   and   the   same   libraries  will  be  offered   sets   of  "Biographies  of 
California  Authors  and  Indexes  of  California  Literature."     It   is  a  great 
satisfaction  to  me  to   announce  the   completion  of  this  voluminous  work  which 
envisioned  in  1936,   now  stands  as  a  splendid  tribute  to   its   sponsor  and  to 
the  ',/ork  Projects  Administration.     The  number  of  enthusiastic   letters  that 
have  been  sent  to  me   seem  to   show  that   the    field  of  0-iliforniana  has  teen 
truly  enriched  by  this   contribution. 

While   I   know   and    fully  appreciate  the  hard  work  done  by  a  loycl  and 
capable   staff,  I    feel  that  a  special  tribute   is  due  to  Mr.  Ecgar  J.  Hinkel  v/ho 
conceived   this   study  of  California  literature   in  the  beginning,   worked   out   its 
many  details  and  trained  the  personnel  needed   for  its  execution.      It  was 
through  his   efforts   and  the  help   of  his   assistant   editor,   Mr.   William  E.   MoCann, 
that   a  very  difficult  undertaking  was  brought  to  happy   fulfillment. 

The  staffs  of  the    following  libraries  deserve  our  thanks   for  their 
interest   and   encouragement   as  well  as    for  their  liberal  assistance:      California 
State  Library;    University  of  California  Library;   Berkeley  Fublic   Library; 
Bancroft   Library,   University  of  California;    Oakland  Public   Library;    Los  Angeles 
Public   Library;    San  Francisco  Public   Library. 

The  V/ork  Projects  Admini  strati  or   provided  the    funds    for  labor,    and   the 
office  of  the  District  Administration,   and  Mrs.   Phyllis     Xristich  in  particular, 
are  to   be   commended    for  unfailing  cooperation.      A  debt   of  gratitude   is   also  due 
to  the  Board  of  Supervisors   of  Alameda  County  v/ho   each  year  appropriated   the 
sponsor's   contribution   for  operation  of  the  project. 

At   the  time   of  my  retirement    in  November,    1941,   the   last   of  our  eight 
volumes  was  nearing  completion.      It   was  the  end   of  a   long  period  of  pleasant 
collaboration.     May  the  years  to   come  vindicate  my  sincere  belief  that   the 
Alameda  County  Library  has   added   a  unique   stimulus  to   research   in  the    field 
of  California  letters. 


Mary  Barmby,  Librarian   (1910-1941). 
Alameda  County  Library . 
Oakland,   California 
February  2* .   194?. 


PREFACE  ii 


In  1936,  under  the  sponsorship  of  tho  Alameda  County  Library,  an  ambi- 
tious program  of  research  was  outlined  which  was  to  embrace  the  entire  field 
of  California  fiction,  poetry  and  drama.   The  original  program  soon  fell  into 
the  pattern  of  a  trilogy.  Part  one  of  this  trilogy,  "Bibliography  of  Cali- 
fornia Fiction,  Poetry,  Drama"  was  published  in  three  volumes  in  1938. 
Part  two,  "Criticism  of  California  Literature;  a  Digest  and  Bibliography," 
likewise  in  three  volumes,  appeared  in  1940.  Part  three  is  embodied  in  the 
present  volumes. 

As  work  went  forward  en  the  first  two  parts  of  the  trilogy  the  diffi- 
culty of  obtaining  accurate  biographical  data  not  only  justified  the  original 
belief  that  a  comparative  study  of  biographical  sources  would  be  useful,  but 
led  to  the  conviction  that  such  a  study  is  essential  for  scholarly  research  in 
California  letters.  More  than  one  practical  purpose  would  be  served  by  such  a 
comparative  study: 

1.  No  exact  standard  of  reliability  has  been  established  among  works  of 
this  character,  therefore,  a  comparative  study  of  sources  would  be  the  surest 
guide  for  a  student  of  biography. 

2.  Variations  in  statement  of  biographical  detail  (far  more  numerous 
than  might  be  supposed),  would  be  brought  to  light. 

3.  Collation  of  hundreds  of  sources  would  often  result  in  more  complete 
biographies  thun  could  be  found  in  single  sources. 

4.  Facts  would  appear  regarding  numerous  minor  authors  whose  biographies 
are  generally  neglected. 

5.  Reference  librarians  would  be  provided  with  an  invaluable  instrument 
for  helping  those  engaged  in  California  literary  research. 

6.  Western  literature  would  be  enriched  with  a  work  devoted  exclusively 
to  California  authors  who  have  published  fiction,  poetry  or  drama. 

These  considerations,  and  the  enthusiastic  approval  of  librarians,  en- 
couraged the  Alameda  County  Library  to  undertake  this  third  part  of  the  trilogy 
in  June,  1940. 

Through  exhaustive  research,  a  list  of  365  biographical  sources  was  com- 
piled from  the  files  of  the  California  State  Library,  the  libraries  of  the 
University  of  California,  the  Alameda  County  Library  and  the  public  libraries 
of  the  San  Francisco  bay  region.   To  use  these  sources  the  immediate  need  was 
a  complete  catalogue  of  California  authors  whose  biographies  were  to  be  com- 
piled.  Nc  such  catalogue  was  to  be  found  except  in  the  first  part  of  the  tril- 
ogy, "Bibliography  of  California  Fiction,  Poetry,  Drama'',  and  in  the  Supplement 
in  part  two  of  the  trilogy,  "Criticism  of  California  Literature,  a  Digest  and 
Bibliography."  These  two  works  supplied  a  list  of  2,6  77  authors. 


The  next  problem  was  to  determine  procedures  and  to  devise  methods 
for  research.  A  blank  form  was  drafted  (see  copy  following  p  307)  by  ex- 
tracting from  all  source  books  those  biographical  items  generally  regarded 
as  essential  and  this  form  was  filled  in  at  libraries  by  research  workers 
carefully  instructed  in  its  use.  Completed  forms  were  submitted  to  the 
chief  filing  clerk  who  kept  a  "control"  file  for  each  author.  When  this 
biographical  research  came  to  an  end,  the  editorial  staff  began  the  enormous 
task  of  collating  from  15,000  research  forms  information  concerning  2677 
authors. 

The  original  research  forms  used  in  compiling  the  biographies  listed 
in  this  work  may  be  consulted  at  the  Alameda  County  Library,  Oakland, 
California. 

Innumerable  variations  in  almost  every  detail  on  the  research  form 
confronted  the  editors,  but  thoy  faced  a  more  baffling  problem  in  attempt- 
ing to  reconcile  details  superficially  similar  but  differing  vaguely  in 
certain  particulars.   In  these  instances  supplementary  research  was  the 
only  recourse  and  this,  together  with  the  original  research,  kept  a  staff 
of  twenty  trained  persons  busy  over  a  period  of  two  years. 

Authors  vrtiose  biographies  appear  in  this  work  are  divided  into  two 
categories:   1.  Native-born  California  authors  and  those  who  resided  ten 
or  more  years  in  the  State.   The  lives  of  this  group  are  presentod  in  every 
detail  that  was  to  be  found  in  source  books.   2.   Authors  who  lived  less 
than  ten  years  in  California  or  who  never  lived  in  the  State  but  wrote 
fiction,  poetry  or  drama  about  it.   Information  concerning  this  group  is 
limited  to  a  list  of  sources  from  which  biographical  data  may  be  obtained 
and,  for  authors  who  have  lived  in  California,  the  place  and  length  of 
residence. 

The  second  volume  of  the  present  work  is  devoted  entirely  to  two 
indexes  of  California  Literature:   1.  A  complete  index  of  titles  that  appear 
in  the  "Bibliography  of  California  Fiction,  Poetry,  Drama"  (1938),  and  in  the 
supplement  in  the  ''Criticism  of  California  Literature;  a  Digest  and  Biblio- 
graphy" (1940).    2.   A  chronological  index  listing  the  same  titles  under  the 
year  of  publication.  The  earliest  date  that  could  be  found  was  chosen — either 
the  date  listed  by  the  Library  of  Congress  or  a  date  derived  from  the  files  of 
some  California  library.  As  far  as  can  be  learned,  this  is  the  first  chrono- 
logical index  of  California  literature  to  be  compiled.   (See  note  preceding 
Chronological  Index  in  v.  2) 

The  editors  consulted  librarians  before  deciding  upon  format  and 
agreed  with  them  about  certain  details  such  as  mimeographing  on  only  one  side 
of  the  sheet  and  the  minimum  use  of  punctuation.   The  blank  side  of  sheets 
will  prove  useful  to  librarians  who  wish  to  keep  the  volumes  up  to  date. 


The  editors  are  glad  of  this  opportunity  to  express  their  sincere 
appreciation  of  the  conscientious  efforts  of  twenty  assistants  who 
carried  the  burden  of  the  work  through  endless  details  from  first 
research  to  final  publication.  Bernice  Bernard  directed  the  routing  and 
filing  of  forms;  Emil  Bunje,  Ph.  D.  ,  Norman  Tyler,  and  Hero  Rensch,  M.  A., 
were  editorial  assistants;  Edith  Benedict  helped  in  final  compilation,  and 
James  II.  Shelton  was  nimeographer. 

The  editors  shall  remember  with  pleasure  many  hours  of  collaboration 
with  Miss  Mary  Barmby,  formerly  Librarian  of  the  Alameda  County  Library  and 
sponsor's  representative,  who  shared  with  them  her  time  and  the  fruit  of 
her  long  experience. 


Edgar  J.  Hinkel 
William  E.  McCann 


Oakland,  California 
rebruary  26,  1942

SYMBOLS  kW   EXPLANATORY  NOTES  vi 

(*)  Asterisk  signifies  that  the  author  was  born  in  California. 

Code  symbols  such  as  F36  P10  D16  S(D)3  following  author's  name.   These  refer 
to  volume  and  page  in  "Bibliography  of  California  Fiction,  Poetry,  Drama" 
'1938),  and  the  supplement  S,  in  "Criticism  of  California  Literature,  A  Di- 
gest and  Bibliography"  (1940),  where  authors'  complete  works  are  to  be  found 
with  descriptive  lines  and  California  locale. 

i_c]  following  author's  name  or  pseudonym.   This  indicates  correction  of  spell- 
ing or  data  added  to  what  appears  in  "Bibliography  of  California  Fiction,  . 
Poetry,  Drama"  and  its  supplement  in  "Criticism  of  California  Literature, 
A  Digest  and  Bibliography." 

Numbers  in  brackets  [24,  32,  47j  after  a  statement  of  fact  indicate  sources 
of  biographical  data.   To  identity  the  sources  so  indicated,  the  reader 
should  refer  to  the  Bibliography  of  Sources  (arranged  numerically)  and  for 
complete  bibliographical  data  to  the  Bibliography  of  Sources  (arranged  al- 
phabetically).  See  Table  of  Contents. 

b    born 

n   fnom  de  plume)  pseudonym 

d    died 

p    parents 

m    married 

c    children 

e    education 

f    fraternity  (or  sorority) 

s    societies  (and  clubs) 

a    awards 

w    war  record  or  military  service 

v   ("vote")  political  affiliation  or  activity 

t    ("theological")  religious  denomination  or  activity 

h    hobbies  (and  sports) 

j    '"journeys")  foreign  travel 

o  occupations  and  avocations  ( occupations  such  as  playright,  novelist,  poet 
are  not  given  under  "o"  as  this  information  appears  in  the  code  follow- 
ing author's  name.   (See  second  explanatory  note  at  top  of  page). 

r    residence  in  California.   It  was  not  possible  (except  in  very  few  in- 
stances) to  determine  the  length  of  residence  in  each  place.   The  dates 
given  represent  the  entire  time  of  residence  given  in  the  sources.

MASTER  IMDEX  CF  AUTHORS  xii 

There  are  two  complete  alphabetical  arrangements  of  biographies  (and  a  short 
addendum)  in  this  work.  A  Master  Index  of  Authors  is  therefore  necessary  to  dis- 
tinguish at  a  glance  whether  a  Riven  author  appears  in  the  first  section,'  native 
born  authors  and  authors  with  ten  or  more  years  of  residence  in  California,  or  in 
the  second  section,  authors  with  less  than  ten  years  residence  or  no  residence  in 
California,  The  numerals  1  or  2  after  an  author's  name  indicate  that  his  biography 
will  be  found  in  the  first  or  second  section,  respectively.  The  letter  A  after  an 
author's  name  indicates  that  his  biography  appears  in  the  addundum  which  precedes 
the  first  section  of  the  work.  To  find  the  two  sections  and  the  addendum  see  the 
Table  of  Contents,

BI0GRATHI3S  OF  CALIFORNIA  AUTHORS 

(Loss  than  ten  years  residence) 

Explanatory  Note 


The  original  purpose  of  this  work  was 
to  compile  from  a  complete  list  of  2 ,677 
authors  the  biographies  of  those  who  were 

native  Calif ornians  or  who  had  lived  ten 
years  or  more  in  tho  state.   In  accomplish- 
ing this  •our^osy,  however,  biographical 
material  was  gathered  for  no  loss  than  917 
authors  who  v/ore  neither  natives  nor 
residents  for  ten  years  but  who  had  written 
fiction,  ooetry  or  drama  with  California 
locale.  It  would  bo  regrettable  to  discard 
those  biographies  even  though  thoy  be  out- 
side the  exact  compass  of  tho  work,  and  are 
a  mere  by-nroduct  of  tho  major  undertaking, 
honco  they  are  offered  in  the  following 
pages  in  a  limited  and  condensed  form. 


v?z 


CALIFORNIA  AUTHORS 
Less  than  ten  years  residence  in  California
